questions.questionText: The most common trigger of erythema nodosum among infections in endemic regions is:
questions.explanation: Erythema nodosum is associated with infections, drugs, and systemic inflammatory disease, including TB.

questions.questionText: A butterfly malar rash sparing nasolabial folds is classically seen in:
questions.explanation: Malar rash with photosensitivity is a classic mucocutaneous feature of SLE.

questions.questionText: Acanthosis nigricans in adults should prompt evaluation for:
questions.optionB: Insulin resistance and occasionally underlying malignancy
questions.explanation: Acanthosis nigricans is strongly linked to insulin resistance and can be paraneoplastic.
Palpable purpura is a hallmark of cutaneous small-vessel vasculitis.

questions.questionText: A painful vesicular dermatomal rash in an elderly patient is most likely:
questions.explanation: Herpes zoster causes unilateral dermatomal painful vesicular eruption.

questions.questionText: Nikolsky sign positivity is classically seen in:
questions.explanation: Pemphigus vulgaris has intraepidermal blistering and positive Nikolsky sign.

questions.questionText: The skin lesion strongly associated with infective endocarditis among the following is:
questions.explanation: Janeway lesions and Osler nodes are classic peripheral stigmata of infective endocarditis.

questions.questionText: A heliotrope rash with Gottron papules is characteristic of:
questions.explanation: Heliotrope rash and Gottron papules are highly characteristic of dermatomyositis.

questions.questionText: Necrolytic migratory erythema is a cutaneous marker of:
questions.explanation: Necrolytic migratory erythema is classically associated with glucagonoma syndrome.

questions.questionText: A non-blanching petechial rash with fever and hypotension should immediately raise concern for:
questions.explanation: Petechial/purpuric rash with shock can indicate meningococcemia, a life-threatening emergency.

questions.questionText: Pyoderma gangrenosum is commonly associated with:
questions.explanation: Pyoderma gangrenosum is a neutrophilic dermatosis often linked to IBD and systemic disease.

questions.questionText: The most important first step in suspected Stevens-Johnson syndrome drug reaction is:
questions.explanation: Prompt discontinuation of the causative drug is critical in SJS/TEN management.

questions.questionText: Toxic epidermal necrolysis (TEN) differs from Stevens-Johnson syndrome (SJS) primarily by:
questions.optionB: Extent of skin detachment (TEN: >30% BSA involved vs SJS: <10% BSA) — TEN is the more severe form with higher mortality
questions.explanation: SJS/TEN spectrum: severe cutaneous adverse drug reactions. Classification by BSA detachment: SJS <10%, SJS-TEN overlap 10-30%, TEN >30%. Both: widespread epidermal necrosis, mucosal involvement (oral, ocular, genital), full-thickness epidermal detachment (positive Nikolsky sign). TEN mortality: 25-35%. Common drugs: carbamazepine, phenytoin, allopurinol, sulfonamides, NSAIDs, nevirapine. HLA associations: HLA-B*15:02 (carbamazepine), HLA-B*58:01 (allopurinol). Management: stop offending drug, ICU/burn unit, supportive care. SCORTEN: prognostic scoring. Reference: Harrison — SJS/TEN.

questions.questionText: Erythema multiforme (EM) is most commonly triggered by:
questions.optionB: Herpes simplex virus (HSV) infection — presenting as target/iris lesions
questions.explanation: Erythema multiforme: acute, self-limited skin condition. Classic lesion: target/iris lesion (3 concentric zones — central dusky/vesicular, pale edematous ring, peripheral erythema). EM minor: skin only, self-limited. Most common trigger: HSV (accounts for ~90% of recurrent EM). Drug-induced EM is less common than previously thought (drugs more commonly cause SJS/TEN). Acral distribution (hands, feet, elbows, knees). Mucosal involvement: EM major. Recurrent EM: prophylactic acyclovir if HSV-triggered. Reference: Harrison — Erythema Multiforme.

questions.questionText: Sweet syndrome (acute febrile neutrophilic dermatosis) is associated with:
questions.optionB: Hematologic malignancies (especially AML), inflammatory bowel disease, and upper respiratory infections
questions.explanation: Sweet syndrome: tender, erythematous plaques/nodules with fever, neutrophilia, and dense neutrophilic infiltrate on biopsy. Associations: (1) Idiopathic (most common), (2) Malignancy-associated (AML — most common paraneoplastic association, MDS), (3) Drug-induced (G-CSF), (4) Inflammatory (IBD, infections). Responds dramatically to corticosteroids. Pathergy may be present. Consider malignancy workup if: atypical/bullous lesions, cytopenias, recurrent episodes. Reference: Harrison — Sweet Syndrome.
